This page depends on the TotpEnrollment service. Since the user is already enrolled (they signed in with TOTP), the code path hits CountRecoveryCodesAsync. Let me read the TotpEnrollment service.

Done
The GET path code is sound. But EnrollTotp.razor depends on a non-null [CascadingParameter] HttpContext — which is only present under static SSR, not an interactive circuit. If this page renders interactively, HttpContext is null and GetUserAsync(HttpContext.User) throws → 500. Let me check how render modes are assigned in App.razor.


Scrutinizing code logic and reconsidering the problem holistically
Scrutinizing code logic and reconsidering the problem holistically

Read App.razor for render mode logic
Diagnosing render mode logic for excluded routing pages.

Scrutinizing code logic and reconsidering the problem holistically.

Done
Render mode is correct ([ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting] → static SSR, so HttpContext is present). Since login works, the container isn't stale, so this is a genuine runtime error unique to this page. The one thing distinct about EnrollTotp.razor versus the working pages is the injected SignInManager<Person>. Let me compare against SignIn.razor (which works) and inspect RestaurantSignInManager's constructor.


Investigated 500 error root cause, narrowed to TotpEnrollment injection issue
Investigated 500 error root cause, narrowed to TotpEnrollment injection issue

Read SignIn.razor to see how it injects/uses SignInManager
Diagnosing the Security page's 500 error discrepancy.

Requesting server logs to diagnose the mysterious 500 error.

Verifying build integrity and pinpointing the 500 error source.

Pinpointing RestaurantName configuration triggering TotpEnrollment resolution failure.

Investigated 500 error root cause, narrowed to TotpEnrollment injection issue.

Done
SignIn.razor injects SignInManager<Person> too and works — so that injection is fine. The real difference: EnrollTotp.razor injects TotpEnrollment, whose constructor does ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrEmpty(issuer) where issuer = options.RestaurantName. If RestaurantName is empty, resolving TotpEnrollment throws → 500 — and this page is one of the few that inject it. Let me check RestaurantOptions and whether /setup (which worked) even constructs TotpEnrollment.


Scrutinizing parameter validation logic and dismissing initial hypothesis
Scrutinizing parameter validation logic and dismissing initial hypothesis

Read RestaurantOptions.cs
Scrutinizing parameter validation logic and dismissing initial hypothesis.

Done
RestaurantName defaults to "My Restaurant", so TotpEnrollment construction never throws. That hypothesis is out too. Let me compare against the sibling account page Passkeys.razor (also in the nav, also static SSR, also calls UserManager.GetUserAsync) — if it shares the exact GET pattern, the bug is narrower than I think.


Traced Security endpoint thoroughly, needs stack trace for diagnosis
Traced Security endpoint thoroughly, needs stack trace for diagnosis

Read Passkeys.razor GET path and injections
Diagnosing untested passkeys routing and potential user manager issues.

Investigating TOTP secret decryption in Security endpoint flow.

Weighing diagnostic necessity against speculative fixes.

Diagnosing Security page 500 error, weighing stack trace necessity.

Weighing stack trace necessity against speculative debugging.

Traced Security endpoint thoroughly, needs stack trace for diagnosis.

Done
Passkeys.razor shares the identical GET pattern (static SSR, injects UserManager/SignInManager, calls UserManager.GetUserAsync). If GetUserAsync were the culprit, both pages would fail — but the user only reported Security. That points to something unique to EnrollTotp. Yet its unique calls (GetTwoFactorEnabledAsync, CountRecoveryCodesAsync) are both sound. Let me confirm one load-bearing assumption: that Person is a class (lenient Dapper binding), not a positional record — since my "sign-in works ⇒ FindByIdAsync works" reasoning depends on it.


Deliberated stack trace necessity before proceeding with fixes
Deliberated stack trace necessity before proceeding with fixes

Read the Person class definition
Unraveling the elusive bug lurking beneath the surface.

Excavating stack traces to pinpoint the Security page error.

Requesting stack trace to diagnose Security page error.

Deliberated stack trace necessity before proceeding with fixes.

Done
I've now traced the entire /account/enroll-totp request path, and this is where I need to stop and be straight with you rather than guess.

Good news first: login working end-to-end (password + TOTP) tells me a lot. It means the container is running current code (not stale), the DB and migrations are fine, Data Protection can unprotect the admin's TOTP secret, and the claims/GetUserAsync machinery is healthy.

That's exactly why the Security 500 is puzzling. Here's everything I checked on that page's GET path, and every one of them is correct:

Render mode — EnrollTotp.razor has [ExcludeFromInteractiveRouting], so App.razor renders it as static SSR, which means the [CascadingParameter] HttpContext is present (not null). No null-HttpContext crash.
DI — TotpEnrollment resolves fine; its issuer is RestaurantName, which defaults to "My Restaurant", so the ThrowIfNullOrEmpty guard can't fire.
SignInManager<Person> injection — SignIn.razor injects the same service and works, so that's not it.
The recovery-code count — CountCodesAsync is a trivial SELECT count(*)::int … WHERE used_at IS NULL, returning an int. The table exists and has rows (your setup bootstrap wrote them).
Person binding — it's a class with property setters, and sign-in reads the same POCO, so the DateTimeOffset/timestamptz issue that bit us last session (which only affects positional records) doesn't apply here.
Obligations middleware — your admin has no outstanding obligations, so it passes straight through.
Passkeys.razor uses the identical GET pattern, so if this were a shared cause, both would fail — but you only hit it on Security. Nothing I can see in the source should throw here.

Since you're running Production (ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production), the browser only shows the bare "Error: 500" — but the real exception and stack trace are printed to the web container's stdout. That one block will pin this in seconds, and it's much better than me shipping a speculative fix that risks adding churn without actually resolving it.

Click Security once more to reproduce, then grab the trace:

bash
podman logs --tail 120 myrestaurant_web_1 2>&1 | sed -n '/fail:/,$p'
Paste the block that starts with the exception type (e.g. System.…Exception) and its    at … stack frames. If that comes back empty, just run podman logs --tail 120 myrestaurant_web_1 and paste the tail.

On sequencing: because I can't size this fix until I see the exception, I'm holding M3 Slice 2 (join-token service + validation + metrics) rather than starting a new feature on top of an unexplained 500 — that keeps the day ending on mostly-working code, which is what you asked for. Once you paste the trace, I'll fix it precisely and, if it's small, fold Slice 2 into the same structured tar.gz (repo-root layout, with an explicit delete list as always).


Now using credits • Your plan limit resets Jul 23 at 6:10 AM.






Claude is AI and can make mistakes. Please double-check responses.
